dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 4 responding for breathing difficulty at 2403, [CITY].
Hi, my name is Maria, and I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been having trouble breathing for about an hour. I feel like I'm not getting enough air.
Do you have a history of breathing problems, like COPD?
Yes, I've had COPD for several years and it's been acting up lately.
Can you describe how your symptoms are different today compared to your usual COPD flares?
I'm not having chest pain, but I've had to increase my oxygen from 5 LPM to 13 LPM using two cannulas to get any relief.
Let me check your mental status. Can you tell me your name, where you are, who I am, and the current day and time?
My name is Tyler, I'm in my apartment in [CITY], you're an EMT, and today is Sunday the 10th.
Thank you. You are alert and oriented to four (AOx4) and scoring 15 on the Glasgow Coma Scale.
Let me listen to your lungs with a stethoscope. Can I proceed?
Your breathing is increased to 22 breaths per minute. That's faster than normal for resting breathing.
I can hear expiratory wheezing in both lungs. I'm going to apply CPAP now to help improve your breathing.
Mr. Lucas, I'll check your pulse now. Your heart rate is 108 beats a minute, but the rhythm is strong and regular.
Let me take your blood pressure now. You're at 171 over 101, which is elevated.
Let me place this monitor on your chest to check your heart rhythm. You're in sinus tachycardia, a fast heart rate due to stress or low oxygen.
When did your breathing difficulties start exactly?
About an hour ago. I gradually increased my oxygen to 13 LPM using two cannulas.
Have you taken any medications so far?
I took my Albuterol inhaler, but it didn't help much. I also took a Nitroglycerin pill just in case, though I've never really had chest pain before.
I'll now check your blood sugar. It reads zero, so we'll continue to monitor.
Can you tell me about any chronic health problems you have?
I have end-stage kidney disease and need regular dialysis. I also had heart bypass surgery and am coping with congestive heart failure.
What medications do you take regularly?
I take medications like Metoprolol for heart issues, Atorvastatin for cholesterol, and Ventolin for breathing issues.
I'm now setting up the CPAP mask and placing the 4-lead ECG monitor. Let me know if you're comfortable with the equipment.
Since your oxygen saturation is low at 85%, we are applying CPAP now to provide more air support.
Your peripheral pulses are strong, regular, and equal on both sides.
Any pain in the head, back, chest, or stomach today?
No pain at all. I just feel weak from my condition. I have a dialysis fistula over the left bicep, but it's not affecting anything at the moment.
I see from your response that you are reporting pain as zero.
Your mental status has not changed since initial assessment. You remain alert and oriented.
I'm going to give you a combination nebulizer treatment called Duoneb with Albuterol and Atrovent. This should help open your airways and make it easier for you to breathe.
The wheezing is still present in both lungs, but it seems slightly improved since the CPAP and nebulizer were started.
Your oxygen saturation has already improved from 85% to 98% with the CPAP-this is a good sign.
Are you experiencing any nausea or vomiting on top of your breath problems?
No vomiting or nausea. I do feel slightly tired and weak like I usually do when my COPD acts up.
We will follow EMS Protocol 3-11 for respiratory distress due to a COPD exacerbation, based on your symptoms and medical history.
I'm now giving you a 10mg intramuscular Dexamethasone shot to help reduce airway inflammation and further improve your breathing.
I will take your vitals again now.
Let's check how effective the Duoneb and CPAP were in helping with your breathing.
Your blood sugar is now at 93, up from the initial zero, which is a good sign of overall improvement.
Your EKG now shows sinus rhythm with a steady pulse at 93. Your BP is now 149 over 91, and your respiratory rate has dropped to 20 per minute.
Your latest respiratory rate is 18 breaths per minute. That's a gradual improvement and a sign of stabilization.
Based on your improvement and medical needs, we're transporting you to [COMPANY_NAME], which is well-equipped for your COPD, CHF, and kidney issues.
Mr. Lucas, would you be willing to assist in moving you to the stair chair for safe transport?
Yes, I'm ready when you are.
We are now carefully placing you on the chair and strapping you securely. We will be extra careful not to place pressure on your dialysis arm.
You are now secured onto the stretcher and in a Fowler's position for maximum comfort during transport.
How are you feeling now compared to when we first met?
I feel a lot better now. Actually, I'm so relaxed I think I could fall asleep now.
That's excellent to hear. Our goal is to get you to [COMPANY_NAME] as quickly and safely as possible to continue recovery.
I'm informing the [COMPANY_NAME] emergency room of your condition and injuries so they can be ready when we arrive.
Right now, your breathing is stable with improved oxygen saturation at 99%. Your condition is well-controlled for transport.
Let's check one more time that the ambulance is fully ready for departure.
Your latest vital signs show a blood pressure of 155 over 90, pulse 88, and oxygen saturation 98%. Improvement is consistent and positive.
They know about your kidney disease, hyperlipidemia, and ongoing respiratory issues before you arrive to ensure optimal hospital care.
The CPAP and oxygen equipment is working, and we will continue monitoring you during transport.
We are fully prepped and ready for a safe trip to [COMPANY_NAME].
Everything in the ambulance is secured, and we have all the necessary care in place for a successful transport.
